questions.question: What is the supreme law of India?
questions.explanation: The Constitution of India is the supreme law of the land. All other laws must be consistent with it.

questions.question: How many fundamental rights are guaranteed by the Indian Constitution?
questions.explanation: The Indian Constitution guarantees 6 Fundamental Rights under Part III (Articles 12-35).

questions.question: Who is known as the Father of the Indian Constitution?
questions.explanation: Dr. B.R. Ambedkar was the Chairman of the Drafting Committee of the Indian Constitution and is called its Father.

questions.question: On which date did the Indian Constitution come into force?
questions.explanation: The Indian Constitution came into force on 26 January 1950, celebrated as Republic Day.

questions.question: Who is the head of the State Government in India?
questions.explanation: The Governor is the constitutional head of each State in India, appointed by the President.

questions.question: What is the minimum age to vote in Indian elections?
questions.explanation: The 61st Constitutional Amendment (1988) reduced the voting age from 21 to 18 years.

questions.question: Which body conducts elections in India?
questions.explanation: The Election Commission of India (ECI) is an autonomous constitutional body that administers elections at the national and state level.

questions.question: Who appoints the Chief Minister of a State?
questions.explanation: The Governor appoints the Chief Minister, who must enjoy the confidence of the Legislative Assembly.

questions.question: The Indian Parliament consists of how many Houses?
questions.explanation: Indian Parliament has two Houses: Lok Sabha (Lower House) and Rajya Sabha (Upper House), plus the President.

questions.question: Who is the ex-officio Chairman of the Rajya Sabha?
questions.explanation: The Vice President of India is the ex-officio Chairman of the Rajya Sabha.

questions.question: How many languages are listed in the Eighth Schedule of the Indian Constitution?
questions.explanation: The Eighth Schedule lists 22 official languages of India.

questions.question: The Rajya Sabha is also called the:
questions.explanation: Rajya Sabha is officially called the 'Council of States'. Lok Sabha is called the 'House of the People'.

questions.question: Which article of the Constitution abolishes untouchability?
questions.explanation: Article 17 abolishes untouchability in any form and makes its practice a punishable offence.

questions.question: What is the term of a Rajya Sabha member?
questions.explanation: Rajya Sabha members serve 6-year terms. One-third retire every 2 years. Rajya Sabha is a permanent body.

questions.question: Which is the highest court in India?
questions.explanation: The Supreme Court of India is the apex court and the final interpreter of the Constitution.

questions.question: Right to Education is guaranteed under which article?
questions.explanation: Article 21A (added by 86th Amendment, 2002) provides free and compulsory education to children aged 6-14 years.

questions.question: Which amendment gave constitutional status to Panchayati Raj institutions?
questions.explanation: The 73rd Constitutional Amendment (1992) gave constitutional status to Panchayati Raj institutions (local self-government in rural areas).

questions.question: How long is the term of the President of India?
questions.explanation: The President of India serves a 5-year term and can be re-elected.

questions.question: What is the national animal of India?
questions.explanation: Bengal Tiger (Panthera tigris tigris) is the national animal of India.

questions.question: Which article empowers the Parliament to create new States or alter the boundaries of existing States?
questions.explanation: Article 3 empowers Parliament to form new States by separation, union, or merger of existing States, or by altering boundaries, names, or areas.

questions.question: Under which article can the President proclaim a National Emergency?
questions.explanation: Article 352 empowers the President to proclaim a National Emergency if the security of India is threatened by war, external aggression, or armed rebellion.

questions.question: Which writ is issued by a court to compel a public authority to perform its legal duty?
questions.explanation: Mandamus (meaning 'We command') is issued to a public authority, lower court, or corporation to perform a public or statutory duty.

questions.question: Which writ is used to produce a detained person before a court?
questions.explanation: Habeas Corpus (meaning 'You may have the body') is issued to free a person from illegal detention.

questions.question: The Directive Principles of State Policy are contained in which Part of the Constitution?
questions.explanation: Directive Principles of State Policy are in Part IV (Articles 36-51). They are non-justiciable but fundamental in governance.

questions.question: How many schedules does the Indian Constitution have?
questions.explanation: The Indian Constitution originally had 8 schedules; it now has 12 schedules after various amendments.

questions.question: Which schedule of the Constitution deals with anti-defection law?
questions.explanation: The Tenth Schedule (added by the 52nd Amendment, 1985) contains provisions for disqualification on grounds of defection.

questions.question: Article 21 of the Indian Constitution guarantees:
options: Protection of Life and Personal Liberty
questions.explanation: Article 21 states: 'No person shall be deprived of his life or personal liberty except according to procedure established by law.'

questions.question: The Right to Property was removed from the list of Fundamental Rights by which amendment?
questions.explanation: The 44th Constitutional Amendment (1978) removed the Right to Property from Fundamental Rights and made it a legal right under Article 300A.

questions.question: How many members can the President nominate to the Rajya Sabha?
questions.explanation: The President nominates 12 members to Rajya Sabha who have distinguished themselves in literature, science, art, or social service.

questions.question: Which constitutional body is known as the 'watchdog of public finance'?
questions.explanation: The Comptroller and Auditor General (CAG), under Article 148, audits all government accounts and is called the watchdog of public finance.

questions.question: Under which article can the President impose President's Rule in a State?
questions.explanation: Article 356 (President's Rule) is invoked when the constitutional machinery of a State fails. It is also called State Emergency.

questions.question: How many Fundamental Duties are listed in the Indian Constitution?
questions.explanation: There are 11 Fundamental Duties. Initially 10 were added by the 42nd Amendment (1976); the 11th (related to education for children) was added by the 86th Amendment (2002).

questions.question: The concept of 'Judicial Review' in India means:
options: The judiciary can declare laws unconstitutional
questions.explanation: Judicial Review gives the Supreme Court and High Courts the power to examine legislation and executive actions and declare them void if they violate the Constitution.

questions.question: Which article of the Indian Constitution deals with the formation of new States?
questions.explanation: Article 3 empowers Parliament to admit new States, form new States from existing ones, alter boundaries or change names.

questions.question: The 42nd Constitutional Amendment (1976) is called:
questions.explanation: The 42nd Amendment is called the 'Mini Constitution' because it made extensive changes, including adding 'Socialist', 'Secular', and 'Integrity' to the Preamble.

questions.question: Which article guarantees freedom of speech and expression in India?
questions.explanation: Article 19(1)(a) guarantees all citizens the right to freedom of speech and expression.

questions.question: The Seventh Schedule of the Indian Constitution deals with:
options: Distribution of powers between Centre and States
questions.explanation: The Seventh Schedule contains three lists — Union List, State List, and Concurrent List — which distribute legislative powers between the Centre and States.

questions.question: What is the minimum age for a person to become a member of Rajya Sabha?
questions.explanation: A person must be at least 30 years old to be elected to the Rajya Sabha.

questions.question: How is the Speaker of Lok Sabha elected?
options: Elected by all Lok Sabha members
questions.explanation: The Speaker of Lok Sabha is elected from among the members of the Lok Sabha by its members.

questions.question: Which amendment reduced the voting age from 21 to 18 years?
questions.explanation: The 61st Constitutional Amendment Act (1988) reduced the voting age from 21 to 18 years.

questions.question: The Indian Constitution was adopted on:
questions.explanation: The Indian Constitution was adopted by the Constituent Assembly on 26 November 1949 (Constitution Day/Samvidhan Divas). It came into force on 26 January 1950 (Republic Day). The Constituent Assembly took 2 years, 11 months, 18 days.

questions.question: The Constituent Assembly was chaired by:
questions.explanation: Dr. Rajendra Prasad was the President (permanent chairman) of the Constituent Assembly. Dr. B.R. Ambedkar was the chairman of the Drafting Committee and is called the 'Father of the Indian Constitution'.

questions.question: The Constitution of India originally had:
questions.explanation: The original Constitution (1950) had 395 articles, 22 parts, and 8 schedules. After amendments, it now has 448 articles, 25 parts, and 12 schedules. It is the world's lengthiest written constitution.

questions.question: India borrowed the concept of 'Fundamental Rights' from:
questions.explanation: India borrowed the concept of Fundamental Rights from the US Constitution (First 10 Amendments — Bill of Rights). The idea of judicial review was also borrowed from the USA.

questions.question: The concept of 'Directive Principles of State Policy' was borrowed from:
questions.explanation: DPSP (Part IV, Articles 36–51) was borrowed from the Irish Constitution (Éire) of 1937. DPSPs are non-justiciable (not enforceable by courts) but fundamental to governance.

questions.question: The Preamble to the Indian Constitution begins with:
options: 'We, the People of India, having solemnly resolved...'
questions.explanation: The Preamble begins: 'We, the People of India, having solemnly resolved to constitute India into a Sovereign, Socialist, Secular, Democratic, Republic and to secure to all its citizens JUSTICE, LIBERTY, EQUALITY, and FRATERNITY...'

questions.question: The words 'Socialist' and 'Secular' were added to the Preamble by the:
questions.explanation: The 42nd Constitutional Amendment Act (1976) during Emergency (PM Indira Gandhi) added 'Socialist' and 'Secular' to the Preamble, making India a 'Sovereign, Socialist, Secular, Democratic Republic'.

questions.question: The Preamble is considered to be:
options: A part of the Constitution (Supreme Court's Kesavananda Bharati Case, 1973)
Not a part of the Constitution
questions.explanation: Initially in Berubari Union Case (1960), the SC said Preamble is not part of the Constitution. But in Kesavananda Bharati Case (1973), the SC held that the Preamble IS a part of the Constitution and can be amended (but not the basic structure).

questions.question: Fundamental Rights are enshrined in:
questions.explanation: Fundamental Rights are in Part III (Articles 12–35) of the Indian Constitution. They are justiciable (enforceable by courts). Originally 7, now 6 (Right to Property removed by 44th Amendment 1978, made a legal right under Article 300A).

questions.question: The six Fundamental Rights are:
options: Right to Equality, Right to Freedom, Right against Exploitation, Right to Freedom of Religion, Cultural & Educational Rights, Right to Constitutional Remedies
Right to Property, Right to Education, Right to Equality, Right to Vote, Right to Work, Right to Freedom
Right to Life, Right to Liberty, Right to Property, Right to Religion, Right to Speech, Right to Assembly
Right to Equality, Right to Work, Right to Education, Right to Food, Right to Health, Right to Housing
questions.explanation: The 6 Fundamental Rights: 1) Right to Equality (Art 14-18), 2) Right to Freedom (Art 19-22), 3) Right against Exploitation (Art 23-24), 4) Right to Freedom of Religion (Art 25-28), 5) Cultural and Educational Rights (Art 29-30), 6) Right to Constitutional Remedies (Art 32).

questions.question: Article 14 of the Indian Constitution guarantees:
options: Right to Equality before law and Equal protection of laws
Right to Life and Personal Liberty
questions.explanation: Article 14: 'The State shall not deny to any person equality before the law or equal protection of the laws within the territory of India.' It is available to citizens AND non-citizens.

questions.question: Untouchability is abolished and its practice is forbidden under:
questions.explanation: Article 17: 'Untouchability is abolished and its practice in any form is forbidden. The enforcement of any disability arising out of untouchability shall be an offence punishable by law.' The Protection of Civil Rights Act 1955 penalises untouchability.

questions.question: Article 19 guarantees freedom to all CITIZENS. Which of these is NOT in Article 19?
options: Freedom to reside and settle anywhere in India
Right to life and personal liberty
questions.explanation: Article 19 gives citizens 6 freedoms: speech/expression, peaceful assembly (without arms), forming associations/unions, free movement, residence/settlement, and practising any profession/occupation. Article 21 (Right to Life and Personal Liberty) is separate.

questions.question: Article 21 guarantees:
options: Protection of life and personal liberty — 'No person shall be deprived of his life or personal liberty except according to procedure established by law'
questions.explanation: Article 21 is one of the most fundamental rights. Through judicial interpretation, it now covers: right to privacy, right to livelihood, right to health, right to clean environment, right to speedy trial, right to dignity, etc. Article 21A guarantees free education for children 6-14 years.

questions.question: The Right to Education (Article 21A) was added by the:
questions.explanation: The 86th Constitutional Amendment Act (2002) inserted Article 21A: 'The State shall provide free and compulsory education to all children of the age of six to fourteen years.' The Right to Education Act 2009 gives effect to this.

questions.question: Article 32 is called the 'Heart and Soul of the Constitution' by Dr. B.R. Ambedkar because:
options: It gives the right to move Supreme Court directly for enforcement of Fundamental Rights — right to constitutional remedies
questions.explanation: Article 32 (Right to Constitutional Remedies) allows any citizen to move the Supreme Court directly if their Fundamental Rights are violated. The SC can issue writs (Habeas Corpus, Mandamus, Prohibition, Quo Warranto, Certiorari). Ambedkar called it the 'very soul of the Constitution'.

questions.question: 'Habeas Corpus' literally means:
options: You may have the body — a writ ordering the production of a detained person before court to determine if detention is lawful
A question of right — challenging a person's claim to public office
It is prohibited — to stop lower court/tribunal from exceeding its jurisdiction
questions.explanation: Habeas Corpus ('you may have the body') is a writ ordering the production of a detained person before a court to determine if the detention is lawful. It protects against arbitrary arrest and detention.

questions.question: The writ 'Mandamus' is issued to:
options: Lower courts to stop exceeding jurisdiction
A public official or body to perform a legal duty they have neglected or refused to perform
A person to justify why they hold a public office
Produce a detained person before court
questions.explanation: Mandamus ('we command') is a writ directing a public official, lower court, government body, or corporation to perform a mandatory public duty that it has failed or refused to perform. Cannot be issued against a private person.

questions.question: Article 44 (Uniform Civil Code) is a:
options: Directive Principle of State Policy — non-justiciable directive to the state to endeavour to secure a UCC for all citizens
questions.explanation: Article 44 (DPSP): 'The State shall endeavour to secure for the citizens a uniform civil code throughout the territory of India.' It is non-justiciable — cannot be enforced through courts. Goa has a common civil code (Goa Civil Code, inherited from Portuguese rule).

questions.question: Directive Principles of State Policy (DPSP) are in:
questions.explanation: DPSPs are in Part IV (Articles 36–51). They are non-justiciable (cannot be enforced by court), but they are 'fundamental to governance.' Inspired by Irish Constitution and the UN's Universal Declaration of Human Rights.

questions.question: The DPSP that requires the state to minimize inequalities in income and status is:
questions.explanation: Article 38 directs the State to secure a social order for promotion of welfare and to minimize inequalities in income, status, facilities, and opportunities. Article 39 lists specific economic principles (equal pay, no concentration of wealth, etc.).

questions.question: Equal pay for equal work for both men and women is under:
questions.explanation: Article 39(d) DPSP: 'equal pay for equal work for both men and women.' Though not a Fundamental Right, the Supreme Court has interpreted Article 39(d) in conjunction with Article 14 and 16 to enforce equal pay.

questions.question: Fundamental Duties were added to the Constitution by the:
questions.explanation: The 42nd Amendment Act (1976) added Fundamental Duties (Part IVA, Article 51A) with 10 duties based on the recommendation of the Swaran Singh Committee. The 86th Amendment (2002) added the 11th duty — to provide opportunities for education to children aged 6-14.

questions.question: How many Fundamental Duties are there currently?
questions.explanation: There are currently 11 Fundamental Duties under Article 51A. Originally 10 were added by the 42nd Amendment (1976). The 86th Amendment (2002) added the 11th duty: for parents/guardians to provide opportunities for education to children aged 6-14.

questions.question: The Indian Parliament consists of:
options: President, Lok Sabha, and Rajya Sabha
Lok Sabha, Rajya Sabha, and Supreme Court
questions.explanation: The Indian Parliament consists of the President + Lok Sabha (House of the People) + Rajya Sabha (Council of States). Article 79. The President is an integral part of Parliament (though not a member of either House).

questions.question: The maximum strength of the Lok Sabha is:
options: 552 (530 elected from states + 20 from UTs + 2 Anglo-Indians, but Anglo-Indian nomination abolished in 2020)
questions.explanation: Maximum strength: 552 (Article 81): 530 members from states + 20 from Union Territories + (formerly 2 Anglo-Indian nominated members, abolished by 104th Amendment, 2020). Current effective strength: 543 elected seats.

questions.question: The term of the Lok Sabha is:
options: 5 years (can be extended during National Emergency)
questions.explanation: The normal term of the Lok Sabha is 5 years from the date of its first sitting after a general election (Article 83). During a National Emergency (Article 352), Parliament can extend its term by 1 year at a time by law.

questions.question: The Rajya Sabha is a permanent body because:
options: It is never dissolved — only 1/3 of its members retire every 2 years
It has more powers than Lok Sabha
Its members are elected for 10 years
questions.explanation: Rajya Sabha is a permanent body (Article 83) — it is never dissolved. Each member serves a 6-year term, and 1/3 of its members retire every 2 years. Currently has 245 members (238 elected + 12 nominated by President for expertise in arts, science, literature, social service).

questions.question: A Money Bill can only be introduced in:
questions.explanation: A Money Bill (Article 110) can only be introduced in the Lok Sabha. After Lok Sabha passes it, it is sent to Rajya Sabha which can only make recommendations (not amend/reject). If no action in 14 days, it is deemed passed. Lok Sabha has final say on Money Bills.

questions.question: The Speaker of the Lok Sabha is elected by:
options: Members of Lok Sabha from among themselves
questions.explanation: The Speaker of the Lok Sabha is elected by the members of the Lok Sabha from among themselves (Article 93). The Speaker maintains order, presides over proceedings, and is the guardian of the powers and privileges of the House.

questions.question: Question Hour in Parliament is held:
options: During the first hour of every sitting day (11 AM-12 noon)
questions.explanation: Question Hour is the first hour (11 AM-12 noon) of each Parliamentary sitting. Members can ask oral questions (starred — answered orally, supplementaries can follow) and written questions (unstarred — written answers). Zero Hour immediately follows.

questions.question: A 'No Confidence Motion' can be moved against:
options: The Council of Ministers (the Cabinet) in the Lok Sabha
questions.explanation: A No Confidence Motion (or Motion of No Confidence) is moved in the Lok Sabha against the Council of Ministers. If passed, the Prime Minister and Cabinet must resign. Requires support of at least 50 Lok Sabha members to be admitted.

questions.question: The President of India is elected by:
options: Elected members of both Houses of Parliament + elected members of state Legislative Assemblies (Electoral College)
questions.explanation: The President is elected by an Electoral College (Article 54): elected MPs (Lok Sabha + Rajya Sabha) + elected MLAs of all state Legislative Assemblies + elected MLAs of Delhi and Puducherry Legislative Assemblies. Single Transferable Vote with proportional representation.

questions.question: The Vice-President of India is elected by:
options: Members of Electoral College (same as President)
Members of both Houses of Parliament (Lok Sabha + Rajya Sabha) — not state legislatures
questions.explanation: The Vice-President is elected by members of both Houses of Parliament (Article 66) — Lok Sabha and Rajya Sabha. State legislators are NOT part of this electoral process (unlike President's election).

questions.question: The Prime Minister is appointed by:
options: President of India — who appoints the leader of the majority party/coalition in Lok Sabha
questions.explanation: The President appoints the Prime Minister (Article 75). Conventionally, the President appoints the leader of the party/coalition that commands majority in the Lok Sabha. The PM is the head of the Council of Ministers and real executive head.

questions.question: The President of India can declare National Emergency under:
options: Article 352 — if national security is threatened by war, external aggression, or armed rebellion
questions.explanation: Article 352 allows the President to proclaim National Emergency if the security of India is threatened by war, external aggression, or 'armed rebellion' (changed from 'internal disturbance' by 44th Amendment). Cabinet must advise in writing. Requires 2/3 special majority in Parliament.

questions.question: President's Rule (State Emergency) is imposed under:
options: Article 356 (if state government cannot function according to Constitution — on Governor's report or otherwise)
questions.explanation: Article 356 (President's Rule/State Emergency): The President can take over state government if state constitutional machinery fails. Proclaimed on Governor's report (or other information). Parliament must approve every 6 months. Maximum: 3 years.

questions.question: The Supreme Court of India was established on:
questions.explanation: The Supreme Court of India was established on 28 January 1950. The Federal Court of India (1937–1950) was its predecessor. The first Chief Justice was H.J. Kania.

questions.question: A judge of the Supreme Court can be removed by:
options: President on the recommendation of PM
An address of Parliament (both Houses) by special majority presented to the President (impeachment)
questions.explanation: A Supreme Court judge can only be removed by the President on an address of Parliament — both Lok Sabha and Rajya Sabha must pass the resolution by a special majority (majority of total membership + 2/3 of members present and voting). This is called impeachment.

questions.question: The principle of 'Judicial Review' allows courts to:
options: Examine and strike down laws/executive actions that violate the Constitution
questions.explanation: Judicial Review (borrowed from the USA) allows Indian courts (especially SC and HCs) to examine the constitutional validity of legislation and executive actions, and strike them down if they violate the Constitution. Articles 13, 32, 226 provide for this.

questions.question: India is described as:
options: A Union of States (quasi-federal — strong centre, federal features)
questions.explanation: Article 1 describes India as 'a Union of States'. India has federal features (two levels of government, written constitution, division of powers, independent judiciary) but also strong unitary features (single citizenship, integrated judiciary, emergency powers). K.C. Wheare called it 'quasi-federal'.

questions.question: The Seventh Schedule of the Constitution contains:
options: Three lists — Union List (97 subjects), State List (66 subjects), Concurrent List (47 subjects)
questions.explanation: The Seventh Schedule (Article 246) contains three legislative lists: Union List (97 subjects on which only Parliament can legislate), State List (66 subjects for states), and Concurrent List (47 subjects where both can legislate — in conflict, Union law prevails).

questions.question: The Governor of a state is appointed by:
questions.explanation: The Governor is appointed by the President of India (Article 155). The Governor is the constitutional head of the state (like President at national level) and acts as the agent/representative of the Centre in the state.

questions.question: A State Legislative Assembly (Vidhan Sabha) member must be at least:
questions.explanation: To be a member of a State Legislative Assembly (Vidhan Sabha), a person must be at least 25 years old (Article 173). To vote, the minimum age is 18 years (reduced from 21 by the 61st Amendment, 1988).

questions.question: The 73rd Constitutional Amendment Act (1992) related to:
options: Panchayati Raj institutions — gave constitutional status to 3-tier system
Financial relations between states and centre
questions.explanation: 73rd Amendment (1992) added Part IX (Articles 243-243O) and 11th Schedule to the Constitution, giving constitutional status to Panchayati Raj. Mandated 3-tier system (Village, Intermediate, District Panchayats), elections, reservation for SC/ST/women (33%), 5-year term.

questions.question: The 74th Constitutional Amendment Act (1992) related to:
options: Urban local bodies (Municipalities) — added Part IXA, Articles 243P-243ZG, and 12th Schedule
questions.explanation: 74th Amendment (1992) added Part IXA and the 12th Schedule, giving constitutional status to urban local bodies (Municipal Corporations, Municipal Councils, Nagar Panchayats). 12th Schedule lists 18 functions for municipalities.

questions.question: How many subjects are listed in the 11th Schedule (Panchayati Raj functions)?
questions.explanation: The 11th Schedule (73rd Amendment) lists 29 subjects that may be transferred to Panchayats, including agriculture, animal husbandry, fisheries, education, markets, public health, libraries, etc. The 12th Schedule (74th Amendment) has 18 subjects for Municipalities.

questions.question: The Constitution of India was drafted by the Constituent Assembly in how many days?
questions.explanation: The Constituent Assembly took 2 years, 11 months, and 18 days (December 9, 1946 to November 26, 1949) to draft the Constitution, holding 11 sessions with 165 sitting days.

questions.question: The Constitution of India borrowed the idea of Directive Principles from:
questions.explanation: Directive Principles of State Policy (Part IV, Articles 36-51) were borrowed from the Irish Constitution (1937). The concept was inspired by the Spanish Constitution of 1931.

questions.question: The concept of Fundamental Rights in Indian Constitution is borrowed from:
questions.explanation: Fundamental Rights (Part III) were largely inspired by the Bill of Rights in the US Constitution (First 10 Amendments). The judicial review mechanism also came from the USA.

questions.question: The Parliamentary form of government in India is borrowed from:
questions.explanation: Parliamentary system (Cabinet government, Prime Minister's role, collective responsibility) is borrowed from the British (Westminster) model. Also known as Westminster Model.

questions.question: The concept of 'Concurrent List' in Indian Constitution is borrowed from:
questions.explanation: The three-list system (Union, State, Concurrent) and specifically the Concurrent List was borrowed from the Australian Constitution.

questions.question: India's Constitution is the world's longest written constitution with originally how many articles?
questions.explanation: Originally the Constitution had 395 articles in 22 parts and 8 schedules. Currently it has 448 articles (as amended) in 25 parts and 12 schedules (as of 2023).

questions.question: The Constitution describes India as:
questions.explanation: Article 1 describes India as a 'Union of States' — not federation. This implies the union is indestructible and states cannot secede. India is not a true federation.

questions.question: The original Constitution had how many Schedules?
questions.explanation: Original Constitution (1950) had 8 Schedules. Currently it has 12 Schedules (4 new schedules added by various amendments).

questions.question: Which schedule lists the official languages of India?
questions.explanation: 8th Schedule originally listed 14 official languages. Currently it has 22 languages. Tamil was one of the original 14 languages.

questions.question: The 9th Schedule was added by which Constitutional Amendment?
questions.explanation: 9th Schedule was added by the 1st Constitutional Amendment (1951) to protect certain land reform laws from judicial review. Laws in 9th Schedule are immune from fundamental rights challenge.

questions.question: The Preamble of India's Constitution begins with:
questions.explanation: The Preamble begins with 'WE, THE PEOPLE OF INDIA, having solemnly resolved to constitute India into...' — reflecting popular sovereignty.

questions.question: The words 'Socialist' and 'Secular' were added to the Preamble by:
questions.explanation: The 42nd Constitutional Amendment (1976) added the words 'Socialist', 'Secular', and 'Integrity' to the Preamble during the Emergency period under Indira Gandhi.

questions.question: The Preamble to the Constitution was amended by:
questions.explanation: The Preamble has been amended only once — by the 42nd Constitutional Amendment Act 1976 — which added 'Socialist', 'Secular', and 'Integrity'.

questions.question: 'Justice — Social, Economic, and Political' in the Preamble was inspired by:
questions.explanation: The concept of Social, Economic, and Political Justice in the Preamble was inspired by the Russian Revolution (1917) and the ideals of socialism.

questions.question: The Preamble declares India as a:
questions.explanation: Preamble declares India as a Sovereign, Socialist, Secular, Democratic, Republic (SSSDR). Sovereign=independent; Socialist=social justice; Secular=no state religion; Democratic=elected government; Republic=no hereditary head.

questions.question: Is the Preamble a part of the Constitution?
questions.explanation: Initially in Berubari case (1960) SC said Preamble is not part of Constitution. Later in Kesavananda Bharati case (1973) SC ruled Preamble IS part of the Constitution and can be amended.

questions.question: Right to Education (RTE) is a Fundamental Right under which Article?
questions.explanation: Article 21A (inserted by 86th Amendment, 2002) makes free and compulsory education for children 6-14 years a Fundamental Right. The RTE Act 2009 gives effect to this right.

questions.question: Article 19 guarantees freedom of:
options: Speech, assembly, movement, profession, and more (6 freedoms)
questions.explanation: Article 19 guarantees 6 freedoms: (a) Speech & Expression, (b) Peaceful Assembly, (c) Form Associations, (d) Move freely, (e) Reside anywhere, (g) Practice any profession/trade.

questions.question: The Right to Property was removed as a Fundamental Right by:
questions.explanation: 44th Constitutional Amendment (1978) removed Right to Property from Fundamental Rights (Article 19(1)(f) and Article 31). It became a legal right under Article 300A.

questions.question: Article 32 is called the 'soul of the Constitution' by:
questions.explanation: Dr. B.R. Ambedkar called Article 32 (Right to Constitutional Remedies) the 'heart and soul of the Constitution'. It allows citizens to directly approach the Supreme Court to enforce Fundamental Rights.

questions.question: Freedom of religion is guaranteed by Articles:
questions.explanation: Articles 25-28 deal with Freedom of Religion: Art. 25 (right to profess, practice, propagate), Art. 26 (manage religious affairs), Art. 27 (no tax for religion), Art. 28 (no religious instruction in state schools).

questions.question: Article 17 abolishes:
questions.explanation: Article 17 abolishes untouchability and its practice in any form is an offense punishable by law. The Protection of Civil Rights Act 1955 and SC/ST (Prevention of Atrocities) Act 1989 implement this.

questions.question: Which Fundamental Right is available to both citizens and non-citizens?
questions.explanation: Article 14 (Equality before law) applies to 'any person' — both citizens and non-citizens. Similarly, Articles 20, 21, 21A, 22, 25-28 apply to all persons, not just citizens.

questions.question: Articles 23 and 24 deal with:
questions.explanation: Articles 23-24 (Right against Exploitation): Art. 23 prohibits human trafficking and forced labor (begar). Art. 24 prohibits employment of children below 14 years in factories/mines/hazardous work.

questions.question: Which Fundamental Right cannot be suspended even during Emergency?
questions.explanation: Under Article 359, during Emergency, rights under Article 19 can be suspended, but Articles 20 (protection against conviction for offenses) and 21 (right to life) CANNOT be suspended even during Emergency.

questions.question: Article 15 prohibits discrimination on grounds of:
options: Religion, race, caste, sex, or place of birth
questions.explanation: Article 15 prohibits discrimination by the State on grounds of religion, race, caste, sex, OR place of birth. However, special provisions for women, children, SCs, STs, OBCs are allowed.

questions.question: The 'Golden Triangle' of Fundamental Rights refers to:
questions.explanation: Articles 14 (equality), 19 (freedoms), and 21 (life and personal liberty) are called the 'Golden Triangle' as they are most frequently invoked and mutually reinforce each other.

questions.question: Article 44 of the Constitution deals with:
questions.explanation: Article 44 DPSP directs the state to secure a Uniform Civil Code (UCC) for all citizens — one set of personal laws regardless of religion for marriage, divorce, inheritance.

questions.question: Article 48A (Protection of environment) was added by:
questions.explanation: Article 48A (protection of environment, forests, and wildlife) was inserted by the 42nd Constitutional Amendment 1976. Article 51A(g) imposes corresponding duty on citizens.

questions.question: Which DPSP relates to 'Equal pay for equal work for men and women'?
questions.explanation: Article 39(d) DPSP directs equal pay for equal work for both men and women — aimed at eliminating gender-based wage discrimination.

questions.question: The Minerva Mills case (1980) established that:
options: Parliament cannot destroy Fundamental Rights by amending DPSPs
Preamble is not part of Constitution
questions.explanation: Minerva Mills case (1980): SC held that Parliament cannot destroy the balance between Fundamental Rights and DPSPs — the 42nd Amendment's attempt to give DPSPs absolute primacy was unconstitutional.

questions.question: Fundamental Duties are contained in:
questions.explanation: Fundamental Duties are in Article 51A (Part IVA), added by the 42nd Amendment (1976) based on Swaran Singh Committee recommendations. 11th duty added by 86th Amendment (2002).

questions.question: Originally Fundamental Duties were:
questions.explanation: Originally 10 Fundamental Duties were added by the 42nd Amendment (1976). The 11th Fundamental Duty (provide education to children aged 6-14) was added by the 86th Amendment (2002).

questions.question: Fundamental Duties were added on the recommendation of:
questions.explanation: Swaran Singh Committee (1976) recommended adding Fundamental Duties to the Constitution. The 42nd Amendment implemented 10 of these duties.

questions.question: The maximum gap between two sessions of Parliament is:
questions.explanation: The Constitution requires that Parliament must meet at least twice a year and the gap between two sessions must not exceed 6 months (Article 85).

questions.question: Money Bills can be introduced only in:
questions.explanation: Money Bills (Article 110) can be introduced only in Lok Sabha (Article 109). Rajya Sabha can only delay it for 14 days but cannot amend or reject it.

questions.question: The Rajya Sabha is a:
questions.explanation: Rajya Sabha is a permanent body — it is never dissolved as a whole. One-third of its members retire every two years (6-year term for each member).

questions.question: Maximum membership of Lok Sabha:
questions.explanation: Lok Sabha maximum strength: 552 (530 from states + 20 from UTs + 2 Anglo-Indians nominated by President). Currently operational strength is 543 (2 Anglo-Indian nominated seats abolished by 104th Amendment).

questions.question: The Deputy Speaker of Lok Sabha is elected by:
questions.explanation: The Deputy Speaker of Lok Sabha is elected by the members of Lok Sabha from among themselves (Article 93). Similarly, the Deputy Chairman of Rajya Sabha is elected by its members.

questions.question: Which Parliamentary procedure is associated with 'starred questions'?
questions.explanation: Starred questions (marked with *) require oral answers and are followed by supplementary questions. Unstarred questions require written answers and no supplementary questions are allowed.

questions.question: Zero Hour in Parliament refers to:
questions.explanation: Zero Hour (informal institution) begins at noon (12:00) after Question Hour, allowing members to raise urgent public matters without prior notice to the Speaker.

questions.question: A joint sitting of Parliament is presided over by:
questions.explanation: Joint sitting of both Houses (Article 108) in case of deadlock is presided over by the Speaker of Lok Sabha (or Deputy Speaker in Speaker's absence).

questions.question: The Budget is presented by the:
questions.explanation: The Union Budget (Annual Financial Statement) is presented by the Finance Minister in the Lok Sabha (traditionally on the last working day of February, now changed to February 1).

questions.question: Quorum in the Lok Sabha is:
questions.explanation: Quorum for Lok Sabha (Article 100): 1/10th of total membership = 55 members (out of 543). Similarly for Rajya Sabha: 1/10th = 25 members (out of 250).

questions.question: Which type of majority is required to pass an Ordinary Bill?
options: Simple majority (majority of members present and voting)
questions.explanation: Ordinary Bills require simple majority — majority of members present and voting (not absolute majority of total membership). Constitutional Amendments require special majority.

questions.question: The President's term of office is:
questions.explanation: The President holds office for 5 years (Article 56). Can be re-elected any number of times. Can resign by writing to Vice President.

questions.question: Minimum age to become President of India:
questions.explanation: To be eligible for President: Must be a citizen of India, minimum 35 years of age, qualified to be a member of Lok Sabha (Article 58).

questions.question: The President of India can be impeached for:
questions.explanation: The President can be impeached only for violation of the Constitution (Article 61). The process starts in either House (special majority of 2/3rd) and is ratified by the other House.

questions.question: Which article gives Parliament the power to create new states?
questions.explanation: Article 3 empowers Parliament to form new states, increase/decrease areas, alter boundaries or change names of existing states. Such bills require Presidential recommendation.

questions.question: The minimum number of members required to sign a notice of No-Confidence Motion is:
questions.explanation: A No-Confidence Motion can be introduced in Lok Sabha if at least 50 members support it (notice signed by minimum 50 members). The Speaker then admits the motion.

questions.question: Cut motions on Demands for Grants are of how many types?
questions.explanation: Three types of Cut Motions: (1) Disapproval of Policy Cut (reduce to ₹1), (2) Economy Cut (reduce by specific amount), (3) Token Cut (reduce by ₹100 to ventilate grievance).

questions.question: The concept of 'due process of law' in India was elaborated in:
options: Maneka Gandhi v. Union of India (1978)
questions.explanation: Maneka Gandhi case (1978): SC interpreted Article 21 broadly — procedure for depriving life/liberty must be fair, just, and reasonable (not mere 'procedure established by law' as literally stated).

questions.question: Which case established that Right to Privacy is a Fundamental Right?
questions.explanation: Justice K.S. Puttaswamy v. Union of India (2017): 9-judge bench unanimously held that Right to Privacy is a Fundamental Right under Article 21.

questions.question: Article 13 of the Constitution deals with:
options: Laws inconsistent with or in derogation of Fundamental Rights are void
questions.explanation: Article 13: Any law that takes away or abridges Fundamental Rights is void to the extent of inconsistency. It also prohibits the State from making such laws — foundation of judicial review.

questions.question: The 'Right to be forgotten' relates to:
questions.explanation: 'Right to be forgotten' (right to erasure of personal data from internet) flows from Right to Privacy under Article 21, as elaborated in the Puttaswamy judgment (2017).

questions.question: The Speaker of Lok Sabha vacates office:
options: When new Lok Sabha meets for the first time
questions.explanation: The Speaker vacates office not when Lok Sabha is dissolved, but 'immediately before the first sitting of the new Lok Sabha' (Article 94). The Speaker thus continues for the first sitting of the new House.

questions.question: The maximum size of the Council of Ministers cannot exceed:
questions.explanation: 91st Constitutional Amendment (2003): Council of Ministers strength shall not exceed 15% of total membership of the Lower House (Lok Sabha). Similarly for state cabinets.

questions.question: The 'Kitchen Cabinet' refers to:
options: Informal group of influential advisors to PM
questions.explanation: Kitchen Cabinet is an informal term for a small group of close advisors (not necessarily ministers) who the PM consults informally on important decisions, bypassing the formal Cabinet.

questions.question: The 'Union Cabinet' is different from 'Council of Ministers' in that:
options: Union Cabinet is a smaller body of senior Cabinet Ministers
Council of Ministers has only PM and senior ministers
questions.explanation: Council of Ministers includes Cabinet Ministers, Ministers of State (independent charge), and Ministers of State (no independent charge). Cabinet is a smaller sub-set of senior ministers who collectively decide policy.

questions.question: Rajya Sabha's special power to authorize Parliament to legislate on State subjects is under:
questions.explanation: Article 249: If Rajya Sabha passes a resolution by 2/3rd majority that it is necessary in national interest, Parliament can legislate on a State List subject for 1 year (renewable).

questions.question: Rajya Sabha can authorize creation of new All India Services under:
questions.explanation: Article 312: Rajya Sabha can authorize Parliament to create new All India Services (like IAS, IPS, IFS) by 2/3rd majority resolution, if it declares such services as necessary in national interest.

questions.question: 'Stare Decisis' means:
questions.explanation: Stare decisis (Latin: 'stand by what has been decided') is the doctrine of precedent — courts follow earlier decisions (especially of higher courts) in similar cases.

questions.question: The original jurisdiction of the Supreme Court is laid down in:
questions.explanation: Article 131: Original jurisdiction of SC — disputes between states, or between Centre and states. Article 132-134: Appellate jurisdiction. Article 136: Special Leave Petition (SLP).

questions.question: 'Letter Jurisdiction' of High Courts is under:
questions.explanation: Article 226 gives High Courts power to issue writs (Habeas Corpus, Mandamus, Certiorari, Prohibition, Quo Warranto) for enforcement of Fundamental Rights and other legal rights.

questions.question: Lok Adalats (People's Courts) are established under:
questions.explanation: Lok Adalats are established under the Legal Services Authorities Act 1987 to provide alternative dispute resolution. Awards made by Lok Adalat are deemed decrees and final, not appealable.

questions.question: A political party gets National Party status if:
options: Wins 2% of Lok Sabha seats from 3 states OR recognized in 4 states
questions.explanation: A party is recognized as a National Party if: (a) Secures at least 6% of votes in 4 states and 4 Lok Sabha seats, OR (b) Wins at least 2% of total Lok Sabha seats from at least 3 different states.

questions.question: The Delimitation Commission is established by:
questions.explanation: Delimitation Commission is established by an Act of Parliament (Delimitation Act). It determines boundaries of constituencies for Lok Sabha and State Legislative Assemblies.

questions.question: 'First Past the Post' electoral system used in India means:
options: Candidate with most votes wins even if less than 50%
questions.explanation: First Past the Post (FPTP) or Simple Plurality System: The candidate with the highest number of votes wins, even if they have less than 50% of votes. Used for Lok Sabha and Vidhan Sabha elections.

questions.question: VVPATs (Voter Verifiable Paper Audit Trails) were introduced in India in:
questions.explanation: VVPATs were first used in India in the Nagaland state election in 2013. They were used in all Lok Sabha constituencies from 2019 elections.

questions.question: The S.R. Bommai case (1994) significantly restricted:
questions.explanation: S.R. Bommai v. Union of India (1994): 9-judge bench held that: (a) President's Rule is subject to judicial review, (b) Floor test must be held before dismissing state governments, (c) Secularism is Basic Structure.

questions.question: Inter-State River Water Disputes are adjudicated by:
options: Tribunals under Article 262 and Inter-State Water Disputes Act
questions.explanation: Article 262 empowers Parliament to provide for adjudication of disputes relating to use, distribution, or control of inter-state rivers. Parliament enacted the Inter-State River Water Disputes Act 1956.

questions.question: Fifth Schedule of the Constitution deals with:
options: Administration of Scheduled Areas and Scheduled Tribes
questions.explanation: 5th Schedule: Administration and control of Scheduled Areas and Scheduled Tribes in states (most states). 6th Schedule: Administration of Tribal Areas in Assam, Meghalaya, Tripura, Mizoram.

questions.question: The PESA Act (1996) extended Panchayati Raj to:
questions.explanation: Panchayats (Extension to Scheduled Areas) Act 1996 (PESA): Extended modified Panchayati Raj provisions to Scheduled Tribal Areas in 10 states with provisions to protect tribal customs and traditions.

questions.question: The National Commission for Backward Classes is established under:
questions.explanation: 102nd Constitutional Amendment (2018) inserted Article 338B, giving constitutional status to the National Commission for Backward Classes (NCBC). Previously it was a statutory body under 1993 Act.

questions.question: The Planning Commission was replaced by:
questions.explanation: NITI Aayog (National Institution for Transforming India) replaced the Planning Commission on 1 January 2015. Unlike Planning Commission, NITI Aayog is an advisory body without power to allocate funds.

questions.question: The Central Vigilance Commission (CVC) was established by:
options: Executive order in 1964 and later CVC Act 2003
questions.explanation: CVC was established by executive order in 1964 (on recommendation of Santhanam Committee on Corruption Prevention). It was given statutory status by the CVC Act 2003.

questions.question: The Advocate General of a State is appointed by:
questions.explanation: Article 165: Advocate General is appointed by the Governor. The Advocate General is the chief law officer of the state, analogous to the Attorney General of India at the Union level.

questions.question: The Attorney General of India is appointed by:
questions.explanation: Article 76: Attorney General is appointed by the President (on advice of the Council of Ministers). The AG must be qualified to be a judge of the Supreme Court.

questions.question: Which writ is known as 'Bulwark of personal liberty'?
questions.explanation: Habeas Corpus (Latin: 'to have the body') is called the great writ/bulwark of personal liberty. It directs the detaining authority to produce the detained person before court and justify detention.

questions.question: Writ of Prohibition is issued to:
options: Prohibit a lower court from exceeding jurisdiction
questions.explanation: Writ of Prohibition is issued to prevent a lower court or tribunal from exceeding its jurisdiction or usurping jurisdiction it does not have. It is issued before judgment.

questions.question: Can High Courts issue all 5 writs?
options: Yes, under Article 226 (and for FRs under Article 226)
No, only SC can issue all 5
HC can issue only Habeas Corpus
HC can issue writs only for FRs
questions.explanation: Article 226 gives High Courts power to issue all 5 writs — not just for enforcement of Fundamental Rights but also for 'any other purpose'. SC under Article 32 issues writs only for FR enforcement.

questions.question: The Central Information Commission (CIC) was established under:
questions.explanation: Central Information Commission was established under the Right to Information Act 2005 to hear appeals and complaints related to public information. Chief Information Commissioner and commissioners are appointed by President.

questions.question: National Green Tribunal (NGT) was established in:
questions.explanation: National Green Tribunal Act 2010 established the NGT for effective and speedy disposal of cases relating to environmental protection and conservation of forests.

questions.question: The Inter-State Council is chaired by:
questions.explanation: The Inter-State Council (Article 263) is chaired by the Prime Minister. It consists of Chief Ministers of all states and 6 Cabinet Ministers of the Union as members.

questions.question: Which Union Territory has its own Legislature and High Court?
questions.explanation: Delhi (National Capital Territory) and Puducherry are the only UTs with their own Legislative Assemblies (and thus elected governments). J&K is now a UT with Legislature after 2019.

questions.question: The status of Delhi as National Capital Territory was given by:
questions.explanation: 69th Constitutional Amendment (1991) gave Delhi the status of National Capital Territory (NCT) with its own Legislative Assembly of 70 seats.

questions.question: Jammu & Kashmir was reorganized into two Union Territories in:
questions.explanation: J&K Reorganization Act 2019 bifurcated the state of J&K into: (1) J&K UT (with legislature), (2) Ladakh UT (without legislature), effective 31 October 2019. Article 370 was also abrogated.

questions.question: Article 370 of the Constitution dealt with:
questions.explanation: Article 370 (now abrogated by Presidential Order in August 2019 ratified by Parliament) gave special autonomous status to J&K, limiting Parliament's legislative powers over J&K.

questions.question: Article 371 deals with:
options: Special provisions for certain states (like NE states)
questions.explanation: Articles 371-371J provide special provisions for certain states: 371A (Nagaland), 371B (Assam), 371C (Manipur), 371D-F (Andhra Pradesh/Telangana), 371G (Mizoram), 371H (Arunachal), 371I (Goa), 371J (Karnataka).

questions.question: 'Privilege Motion' in Parliament is moved against:
options: Ministers/members for breach of parliamentary privilege
questions.explanation: Privilege Motion is moved when a member believes that a minister or another member has committed a breach of parliamentary privilege (e.g., misleading the House, contempt of Parliament).

questions.question: The Constituent Assembly of India was set up under which plan?
questions.explanation: The Cabinet Mission Plan (May 1946) provided for the formation of the Constituent Assembly. Elections to the Constituent Assembly were held in July-August 1946.

questions.question: The Objectives Resolution was moved in the Constituent Assembly by:
questions.explanation: Jawaharlal Nehru moved the Objectives Resolution on 13 December 1946, which outlined the aims and objectives of the Constitution. It was adopted on 22 January 1947.

questions.question: The Constitution of India borrowed the concept of Directive Principles of State Policy from:
questions.explanation: The Directive Principles of State Policy (Part IV) were borrowed from the Irish Constitution of 1937, which itself was influenced by the Spanish Constitution.

questions.question: The concept of 'Rule of Law' in the Indian Constitution was borrowed from:
questions.explanation: Rule of Law, Parliamentary system, Cabinet government, bicameral Parliament, and the Speaker's role were all borrowed from the British Constitution (Westminster model).

questions.question: Article 21 of the Constitution guarantees:
options: Protection of life and personal liberty
questions.explanation: Article 21 states no person shall be deprived of his life or personal liberty except according to procedure established by law. Post-Maneka Gandhi (1978), this procedure must be fair and reasonable.

questions.question: The Right to Education (Article 21A) applies to children in the age group:
questions.explanation: Article 21A (inserted by 86th Amendment, 2002) provides free and compulsory education to children in the age of 6-14 years. The RTE Act 2009 implemented this right.

questions.question: Which Article prohibits employment of children below 14 years in hazardous work?
questions.explanation: Article 24 prohibits employment of children below 14 years in factories, mines or other hazardous employment. The Child Labour (Prohibition & Regulation) Act implements this.

questions.question: Freedom of press in India is derived from which Article?
questions.explanation: Freedom of the press is implicitly included in Article 19(1)(a) — Freedom of speech and expression. The Supreme Court has held press freedom is a part of this fundamental right.

questions.question: Which Fundamental Right was described as the 'heart and soul of the Constitution' by Ambedkar?
questions.explanation: Dr. Ambedkar called Article 32 (Right to Constitutional Remedies) the 'heart and soul of the Constitution' as it gives the right to move the Supreme Court for enforcement of Fundamental Rights.

questions.question: A Money Bill can be introduced in:
questions.explanation: A Money Bill (as defined under Art 110) can be introduced only in the Lok Sabha on the recommendation of the President. Rajya Sabha can only recommend amendments — Lok Sabha may or may not accept.

questions.question: The Rajya Sabha cannot be dissolved because:
questions.explanation: Rajya Sabha is a permanent body — it cannot be dissolved. One-third of its members retire every two years. Its members are elected for 6-year terms by elected members of State Legislative Assemblies.

questions.question: The presiding officer of a Joint Sitting of Parliament is:
questions.explanation: Joint sittings (Art 108) are presided over by the Speaker of Lok Sabha, or in his absence, the Deputy Speaker of Lok Sabha, or if he is also absent, the Deputy Chairman of Rajya Sabha.

questions.question: The 'Question Hour' in Parliament is:
options: The first hour of every parliamentary sitting
The last hour of every sitting
A special session for questions only
questions.explanation: Question Hour is the first hour of a parliamentary sitting (11 AM to 12 Noon in Lok Sabha). Starred questions get oral answers; unstarred questions get written answers.

questions.question: Who can summon and prorogue sessions of Parliament?
options: President on the advice of Cabinet
questions.explanation: The President summons each House of Parliament (Art 85), prorogues the Houses, and dissolves the Lok Sabha — always on the advice of the Council of Ministers (Cabinet).

questions.question: The maximum gap allowed between two sessions of Parliament is:
questions.explanation: Article 85(1) requires that the gap between two sessions of Parliament shall not exceed 6 months. This means Parliament must meet at least twice a year (Budget session and Monsoon/Winter session).

questions.question: Which Article empowers the High Court to issue writs?
questions.explanation: Article 226 empowers High Courts to issue writs (including Habeas Corpus, Mandamus, Prohibition, Certiorari, Quo Warranto) to any person or authority for enforcement of fundamental rights or any other legal right.

questions.question: Which writ is issued to release a person from illegal detention?
questions.explanation: Habeas Corpus (Latin: 'you may have the body') is issued by a court to bring a person before it to determine if the detention is lawful. It is the most important writ for personal liberty.

questions.question: Which writ commands a public authority to perform a public or statutory duty?
questions.explanation: Mandamus (Latin: 'we command') is issued by a superior court to an inferior court, tribunal, or public authority to compel them to perform a ministerial/statutory duty that they have failed to perform.

questions.question: Which writ is issued to challenge the legal authority of a person to hold a public office?
questions.explanation: Quo Warranto (Latin: 'by what authority') is issued to inquire by what authority a person claims to hold a public office. If the claim is found invalid, the person can be ousted from the office.

questions.question: How many times has the National Emergency (Art 352) been proclaimed in India?
questions.explanation: National Emergency was proclaimed 3 times: (1) 1962 — Chinese aggression; (2) 1971 — Pakistan war; (3) 1975 — Internal disturbance (the controversial Emergency under Indira Gandhi).

questions.question: The 42nd Amendment (1976) is called:
questions.explanation: The 42nd Amendment (1976), passed during the Emergency, is called the 'Mini Constitution' as it made sweeping changes — added 'Socialist', 'Secular', 'Integrity' to Preamble, added Fundamental Duties, curtailed judicial review, etc.

questions.question: The voting age in India was reduced from 21 to 18 years by:
questions.explanation: The 61st Constitutional Amendment (1988) reduced the voting age from 21 to 18 years by amending Article 326. India's first Lok Sabha election with 18-year voters was in 1989.

questions.question: The 52nd Amendment (1985) relates to:
questions.explanation: The 52nd Amendment (1985) introduced the Anti-Defection Law by adding the 10th Schedule to the Constitution. It was passed during Rajiv Gandhi's tenure to curb political defections.

questions.question: The 86th Amendment (2002) relates to:
questions.explanation: The 86th Amendment (2002) inserted Art 21A (Right to Education for 6-14 year olds), amended Art 45 (early childhood care and education up to 6 years), and added Art 51A(k) as a parental duty.

questions.question: The Speaker of Lok Sabha is elected by:
questions.explanation: The Speaker of Lok Sabha is elected by the members of Lok Sabha from among themselves (Art 93). The Speaker can be removed by a resolution of the House by a majority of all the then members.

questions.question: The Budget is presented in Parliament by:
questions.explanation: The Union Budget (Annual Financial Statement under Art 112) is presented by the Finance Minister in Lok Sabha. The President causes it to be laid before both Houses.

questions.question: High Court judges retire at the age of:
questions.explanation: High Court judges (Art 217) retire at 62 years of age. Supreme Court judges (Art 124) retire at 65 years. This is different from the 7th Pay Commission recommendation of 65 for HCs.

questions.question: The Governor of a State holds office:
options: During the pleasure of the President
For life tenure like a judge
questions.explanation: The Governor holds office during the pleasure of the President (Art 156). Though the normal term is 5 years, the President (on advice of Cabinet) can remove the Governor at any time.

questions.question: The Finance Commission of India is constituted under:
questions.explanation: The Finance Commission is constituted under Article 280 by the President every fifth year (or earlier). It recommends distribution of taxes between Centre and States and grants-in-aid to States.

questions.question: NITI Aayog replaced which body in 2015?
questions.explanation: NITI (National Institution for Transforming India) Aayog replaced the Planning Commission on 1 January 2015. Unlike Planning Commission, NITI Aayog is a policy think-tank and does not allocate funds directly to States.

questions.question: The 'Doctrine of Basic Structure' means:
options: Parliament cannot amend certain fundamental features of the Constitution
State governments have basic powers that Parliament cannot take away
questions.explanation: The Basic Structure Doctrine (Kesavananda Bharati, 1973) limits Parliament's power to amend the Constitution. Certain features (supremacy of Constitution, republican form, separation of powers, federalism, secular character, judicial review, FRs) form the basic structure and cannot be amended.

questions.question: Article 44 of the Constitution directs the State to secure:
questions.explanation: Article 44 directs the State to endeavor to secure a Uniform Civil Code (UCC) for citizens throughout India. This remains one of the most politically sensitive DPSPs yet to be implemented.

questions.question: India follows the principle of:
questions.explanation: India provides for single citizenship — a person can be a citizen of India only, not of any State. This is unlike the USA where citizens have both national and state citizenship.

questions.question: The Citizenship Amendment Act (CAA) 2019 provides citizenship to persecuted minorities from:
questions.explanation: The CAA 2019 provides a path to Indian citizenship for Hindus, Sikhs, Buddhists, Jains, Parsis, and Christians from Pakistan, Bangladesh, and Afghanistan who entered India before 31 December 2014.

questions.question: The Model Code of Conduct comes into effect from:
options: Date of announcement of elections by Election Commission
questions.explanation: The Model Code of Conduct comes into force from the date of announcement of election schedule by the Election Commission. It remains in force till the completion of the election process.

questions.question: 'NOTA' (None of the Above) option was introduced in Indian elections in:
questions.explanation: The Supreme Court in PUCL v. Union of India (2013) directed the Election Commission to provide NOTA option. NOTA was first used in 5 State Assembly elections in November 2013.

questions.question: Article 1 of the Indian Constitution describes India as:
questions.explanation: Article 1 describes India as a 'Union of States' — not a federation. This term was deliberately chosen to indicate that States have no right to secede from the Union (unlike a federation or confederation).

questions.question: Article 51A of the Constitution deals with:
questions.explanation: Article 51A (Part IV-A) contains the Fundamental Duties of citizens, added by the 42nd Amendment (1976). Originally 10 duties; an 11th duty (relating to education for children) was added by 86th Amendment (2002).

questions.question: Article 368 of the Constitution deals with:
options: Power of Parliament to amend the Constitution
questions.explanation: Article 368 (Part XX) empowers Parliament to amend the Constitution. It provides three methods: simple majority, special majority (2/3rd + majority of total membership), and special majority with ratification by at least half the State Legislatures.

questions.question: Article 17 of the Constitution abolishes:
questions.explanation: Article 17 abolishes untouchability and forbids its practice in any form. Enforcement of any disability arising out of untouchability shall be an offence. The Protection of Civil Rights Act, 1955 implements this.

questions.question: The Constitution of Jammu & Kashmir was abrogated by which action?
options: Presidential Order abrogating Article 370 in August 2019
J&K Reorganization Act 2019 replacing the State with two UTs
Both Presidential Order (abrogating Art 370) and J&K Reorganization Act 2019
questions.explanation: Article 370 was abrogated by Presidential Order on 5 August 2019, and simultaneously the J&K Reorganization Act 2019 bifurcated the State into two Union Territories — J&K (with legislature) and Ladakh (without legislature).

questions.question: The principle 'One Vote, One Value' in Indian elections means:
options: Each vote has equal value — no plural voting
Only one person can vote per family
questions.explanation: The principle of 'One Person, One Vote, One Value' ensures universal adult franchise — each citizen above 18 years has one vote of equal value, regardless of wealth, gender, religion, or caste.

questions.question: The Comptroller and Auditor General of India is appointed by:
questions.explanation: The CAG is appointed by the President of India (Art 148). The CAG can be removed only by the same process as a Supreme Court judge — by an address of both Houses of Parliament (special majority).

questions.question: How many High Courts are there in India currently?
questions.explanation: India currently has 25 High Courts. The latest ones established include the High Court for Telangana (2019) and High Courts for Jammu, Srinagar (after J&K reorganization). Some HCs have benches in multiple cities.

questions.question: The 'Golden Triangle' of the Indian Constitution comprises:
questions.explanation: The 'Golden Triangle' or 'Trinity' of the Constitution comprises Articles 14 (Equality), 19 (Freedoms), and 21 (Life and Personal Liberty). These are considered the most important Fundamental Rights, often read together.

questions.question: India's Constitution is described as 'federal in form but unitary in spirit' because:
options: It has a strong Centre with residual powers and emergency provisions to override States
questions.explanation: India's Constitution has federal features (division of powers, bicameral legislature, independent judiciary) but unitary features dominate: single citizenship, integrated judiciary, strong Centre, emergency provisions that can override States.

questions.question: Which country's constitution is the largest in the world?
questions.explanation: India's Constitution is the largest written constitution in the world — originally with 395 articles, 22 parts, 8 schedules. Currently it has 448 articles (25 parts) and 12 schedules after numerous amendments.

questions.question: Rajya Sabha members who are nominated by the President are experts in:
options: Literature, science, art, and social service
questions.explanation: Article 80(3) says the President nominates 12 members to Rajya Sabha who have special knowledge or practical experience in literature, science, art and social service — to enrich the quality of debate.

questions.question: The term 'sovereign' in the Indian Preamble means:
options: India is free from external control and has supreme power internally
India is under no international obligation
India is independent of all treaties
questions.explanation: 'Sovereign' in the Preamble means India is not subject to any external authority — no foreign power controls India. Internally, the Constitution is supreme. India's membership in Commonwealth or UN does not affect sovereignty.

questions.question: The term 'Republic' in the Preamble means:
options: The head of state is elected, not hereditary
questions.explanation: 'Republic' means the head of state (President) is an elected person and not a hereditary monarch. This distinguishes India from a monarchy like UK where the King/Queen is the constitutional head.

questions.question: The maximum strength of Rajya Sabha is:
questions.explanation: Article 80 states the Council of States (Rajya Sabha) shall consist of not more than 250 members — 238 elected from States and UTs, plus 12 nominated by the President. Currently 245 members (233 elected + 12 nominated).

questions.question: The office of the District Collector/Collector in India was introduced by:
options: British East India Company (Lord Cornwallis system)
questions.explanation: The office of the Collector was introduced by the British East India Company — primarily by Lord Cornwallis through the Permanent Settlement (1793) system in Bengal. The Collector collects revenue and administers the district.

questions.question: How many Fundamental Duties are listed in Article 51A of the Constitution?
questions.explanation: Currently there are 11 Fundamental Duties in Art 51A. The original 42nd Amendment (1976) added 10 duties. The 86th Amendment (2002) added the 11th duty — parents must provide opportunities for education to children aged 6-14.

questions.question: The concept of 'Welfare State' in the Indian Constitution is reflected in:
options: Directive Principles of State Policy (Part IV)
questions.explanation: The Directive Principles of State Policy (Part IV, Arts 36-51) embody the concept of a Welfare State — they direct the State to promote welfare of the people by securing social, economic, and political justice.

questions.question: The Union List contains subjects on which:
options: Both Parliament and States can legislate
Neither Parliament nor States can legislate
questions.explanation: The Union List (97 items) contains subjects on which only the Parliament has exclusive legislative power.

questions.question: What is the maximum gap allowed between two sessions of Parliament?
questions.explanation: The Constitution requires that no more than 6 months should elapse between the last sitting of one session and the first sitting of the next session.

questions.question: Which article gives the President the power to grant pardon?
questions.explanation: Article 72 empowers the President to grant pardon, reprieve, respite, or remission of punishment to persons convicted of offences.

questions.question: The Concurrent List contains subjects on which:
options: Both Centre and States can legislate
questions.explanation: The Concurrent List (52 items) contains subjects on which both the Parliament and State Legislatures can make laws. In case of conflict, Central law prevails.

questions.question: Which article of the Indian Constitution provides for the right to move the Supreme Court for enforcement of Fundamental Rights?
questions.explanation: Article 32 gives the right to move the Supreme Court for enforcement of Fundamental Rights. Dr. Ambedkar called it the 'heart and soul' of the Constitution.

questions.question: Which constitutional amendment introduced the Goods and Services Tax (GST)?
questions.explanation: The 101st Constitutional Amendment Act (2016) introduced GST in India by amending Article 246A and others.

questions.question: Which article of the Constitution provides that the State shall not deny to any person equality before the law or equal protection of the laws?
questions.explanation: Article 14 guarantees equality before the law and equal protection of laws — a core part of Right to Equality.

questions.question: Under which article can the Supreme Court issue writs for enforcement of Fundamental Rights?
questions.explanation: Article 32 empowers the Supreme Court to issue writs (Habeas Corpus, Mandamus, Prohibition, Quo Warranto, Certiorari) for enforcement of Fundamental Rights. Article 226 gives similar power to High Courts.

questions.question: Article 368 of the Constitution deals with the power of Parliament to:
questions.explanation: Article 368 provides the procedure for amendment of the Constitution. Some provisions require simple majority, others a special majority, and some also require ratification by State legislatures.

questions.question: Who was the first President of India?
questions.explanation: Dr. Rajendra Prasad was the first President of India (1950-1962), serving for two consecutive terms.

questions.question: Who was the first Prime Minister of India?
questions.explanation: Pandit Jawaharlal Nehru was India's first Prime Minister, serving from 15 August 1947 until his death on 27 May 1964.

questions.question: Which part of the Indian Constitution deals with Fundamental Rights?
questions.explanation: Part III (Articles 12-35) of the Constitution contains the Fundamental Rights.

questions.question: The Prime Minister of India is appointed by:
questions.explanation: The President appoints the Prime Minister — conventionally the leader of the majority party or coalition in the Lok Sabha.

questions.question: The National Human Rights Commission (NHRC) is established under:
options: The Protection of Human Rights Act, 1993
questions.explanation: NHRC was established under the Protection of Human Rights Act, 1993. Its chairman is always a retired Chief Justice of India.

questions.question: The 74th Constitutional Amendment (1992) is related to:
questions.explanation: The 74th Amendment gave constitutional status to Urban Local Bodies (Municipalities), Nagar Panchayats, Municipal Councils, and Municipal Corporations.

questions.question: The Right to Information Act came into force in:
questions.explanation: The Right to Information Act, 2005 came into force on 12 October 2005. It gives citizens the right to access information held by public authorities.

questions.question: Which article enables the High Courts to issue writs?
questions.explanation: Article 226 empowers every High Court to issue writs including Habeas Corpus, Mandamus, Certiorari, Prohibition, and Quo Warranto for enforcement of Fundamental Rights and for any other purpose.

questions.question: Under the Indian Constitution, 'Residuary Powers' lie with:
questions.explanation: Residuary powers (powers over subjects not mentioned in any of the three lists) vest in Parliament (the Union/Centre) under Article 248.

questions.question: The State Election Commission (for local body elections) was established by:
questions.explanation: The 73rd and 74th Amendments made it mandatory for states to establish State Election Commissions (Article 243K for Panchayats, 243ZA for Municipalities) to superintend, direct, and control elections to local bodies.

questions.question: The Election Commission of India was established on:
questions.explanation: The Election Commission of India was established on 25 January 1950 (one day before the Constitution came into force). 25 January is celebrated as National Voters' Day.

questions.question: The minimum age for voting in India is:
questions.explanation: The voting age was reduced from 21 to 18 years by the 61st Constitutional Amendment Act (1988). This was done to ensure greater youth participation in democracy. India has ~950 million eligible voters (2024).

questions.question: EVM (Electronic Voting Machine) was first used in a general election in India in:
questions.explanation: EVMs were first used in the Goa state assembly elections (1989) on a trial basis. The 1999 General Elections used EVMs across India for the first time. Full nationwide use since 2004 General Elections.

questions.question: The Comptroller and Auditor General (CAG) of India audits:
options: Accounts of the Union government, state governments, and government-owned companies
questions.explanation: The CAG (Article 148–151) audits accounts of Union, states, and all bodies substantially financed from public funds. The CAG report is presented to Parliament/state legislatures. The CAG is appointed by the President and removed by Parliament by the same process as SC judges.

questions.question: The Finance Commission of India is constituted by:
options: President of India every 5 years (Article 280) to recommend how taxes are divided between Centre and States
questions.explanation: The Finance Commission (Article 280) is constituted by the President every 5 years. It recommends: how central taxes are shared with states, grants-in-aid to states, and any other matter referred. The 15th Finance Commission (2021-26) recommended 41% tax devolution to states.

questions.question: UPSC (Union Public Service Commission) conducts examinations for:
options: All India Services and Central Services Group A & B (civil services exam, IAS, IPS, IFS, etc.)
questions.explanation: UPSC (Article 315) conducts Civil Services Examination (IAS, IPS, IFS), combined defence services, engineering services, NDA, etc. for Group A and B Central Services and All India Services. State PSCs conduct state service exams.

questions.question: The National Human Rights Commission (NHRC) was established in:
questions.explanation: NHRC was established on 12 October 1993 under the Protection of Human Rights Act, 1993 (amended 2006). Its Chairperson is a retired Chief Justice of India. It investigates human rights violations, studies treaties, and recommends measures for protection.

questions.question: The procedure for amending the Constitution is in:
questions.explanation: Article 368 deals with the power of Parliament to amend the Constitution. There are three methods: simple majority (certain provisions), special majority (2/3 of members present and voting + majority of total membership), and special majority + ratification by half the state legislatures.

questions.question: Who administers the oath to the President?
questions.explanation: The Chief Justice of India administers the oath to the President (Article 60). In CJI's absence, the senior-most judge of the Supreme Court administers it.

questions.question: The Vice President of India serves as:
