questions.explanation: The Vice President is the ex-officio Chairman of the Rajya Sabha (Article 64). The VP also discharges the functions of the President when the President is unable to do so.

questions.question: The President's power to promulgate Ordinances is under:
questions.explanation: Article 123 empowers the President to promulgate Ordinances when Parliament is not in session. Ordinances have the same force as Acts but must be laid before Parliament within 6 weeks of reassembly.

questions.question: The President acts on the aid and advice of:
options: Council of Ministers headed by PM
questions.explanation: Article 74: Council of Ministers with PM as head shall aid and advise the President, who shall act in accordance with such advice (after 42nd and 44th Amendments, this is binding).

questions.question: Collective responsibility of Council of Ministers means:
options: All ministers are jointly responsible to Lok Sabha
Cabinet is responsible to Rajya Sabha
questions.explanation: Article 75: Council of Ministers is collectively responsible to Lok Sabha — all ministers stand or fall together. A vote of no-confidence against any minister means the entire government falls.

questions.question: A person who is not a member of Parliament can be appointed Minister for maximum:
questions.explanation: A non-member of Parliament can be appointed as Minister but must become a member of either House within 6 months, failing which they cease to be Minister.

questions.question: The Anti-Defection Law is in:
questions.explanation: 10th Schedule (Anti-Defection Law) was added by 52nd Constitutional Amendment 1985 to prevent political defections. Members are disqualified for voluntarily giving up party membership or voting against party whip.

questions.question: A Supreme Court judge can be removed by:
options: Address by both Houses of Parliament followed by Presidential order
questions.explanation: SC judge can be removed only on the grounds of proven misbehavior or incapacity by an address passed by special majority in both Houses of Parliament and Presidential order (Article 124).

questions.question: The retirement age of a Supreme Court judge is:
questions.explanation: Supreme Court judges retire at 65 years (Article 124). High Court judges retire at 62 years. This was the only age limit originally — fixed at 65 by the Constitution.

questions.question: Article 143 empowers the President to seek advisory opinion from:
questions.explanation: Article 143: The President can refer any question of law or fact of public importance to the Supreme Court for its advisory opinion. The SC may give or refuse to give the opinion.

questions.question: 'Public Interest Litigation' (PIL) was developed by:
questions.explanation: PIL was developed by the Supreme Court of India in the 1980s, especially through Justices V.R. Krishna Iyer and P.N. Bhagwati, allowing any citizen to approach courts for public interest.

questions.question: 'Mandamus' writ is issued to:
options: Command a public authority to perform its duty
Transfer a case to another court
questions.explanation: Mandamus (Latin: 'we command') is issued to compel a public authority, inferior court, corporation, or government officer to perform a mandatory duty that they have failed to perform.

questions.question: The writ of 'Quo Warranto' is related to:
options: Challenging a person's legal right to hold a public office
questions.explanation: Quo Warranto (Latin: 'by what authority') questions the legal right of a person to hold a public office. It prevents illegal usurpation of public offices.

questions.question: Under Article 352, National Emergency can be proclaimed on grounds of:
options: War, External Aggression, or Armed Rebellion
questions.explanation: National Emergency (Article 352) can be proclaimed on grounds of: (i) War, (ii) External Aggression, or (iii) Armed Rebellion (formerly 'internal disturbance', changed by 44th Amendment).

questions.question: President's Rule (Article 356) in a state lasts initially for:
questions.explanation: President's Rule (State Emergency) lasts initially for 6 months, can be extended for a maximum of 3 years (with Parliamentary approval every 6 months after first 6 months).

questions.question: Financial Emergency (Article 360) has been invoked in India:
questions.explanation: Financial Emergency under Article 360 has NEVER been proclaimed in India since independence. It can be proclaimed when the financial stability or credit of India or any state is threatened.

questions.question: The 44th Constitutional Amendment (1978) changed the term 'Internal Disturbance' in Article 352 to:
questions.explanation: 44th Amendment (1978) changed 'internal disturbance' to 'armed rebellion' in Article 352, making it harder to impose National Emergency on minor internal disturbances.

questions.question: The minimum age to become Governor is:
questions.explanation: To become a Governor (Article 157): Must be a citizen of India and must have completed 35 years of age. Must not be a member of Parliament or State Legislature.

questions.question: When can the Governor reserve a bill for the President's consideration?
options: When the bill endangers High Court's position or is against Constitution
questions.explanation: Governor can reserve a bill for Presidential consideration if it: endangers the position of the High Court, is repugnant to the Constitution, or against the policy of the Union government.

questions.question: Chief Minister is appointed by:
questions.explanation: Chief Minister is appointed by the Governor. Conventionally, the leader of the majority party/coalition in the State Legislative Assembly is appointed CM. CM need not be a sitting MLA.

questions.question: The Balwant Rai Mehta Committee (1957) recommended:
questions.explanation: Balwant Rai Mehta Committee recommended a three-tier Panchayati Raj structure: (1) Gram Panchayat at village level, (2) Panchayat Samiti at block level, (3) Zila Parishad at district level.

questions.question: The 73rd Constitutional Amendment (1992) relates to:
questions.explanation: 73rd Constitutional Amendment (1992) gave constitutional status to Panchayati Raj institutions, adding Part IX (Articles 243-243O) and 11th Schedule (29 functions transferred to Panchayats).

questions.question: The 11th Schedule of the Constitution relates to:
questions.explanation: 11th Schedule (added by 73rd Amendment, 1992) contains 29 subjects/functions to be assigned to Panchayats — including agriculture, land improvement, rural roads, health, education, etc.

questions.question: 74th Constitutional Amendment (1992) relates to:
questions.explanation: 74th Constitutional Amendment (1992) gave constitutional status to Urban Local Bodies (Municipalities), adding Part IX-A (Articles 243P-243ZG) and 12th Schedule (18 municipal functions).

questions.question: 'Gram Sabha' is a body consisting of:
options: All registered voters in a village
questions.explanation: Gram Sabha is a body consisting of all persons registered in the electoral rolls of the village — the primary democratic unit at grassroots level under the 73rd Amendment.

questions.question: The Election Commission of India is established under:
questions.explanation: Article 324: The Election Commission is a constitutional body with superintendence, direction and control of preparation of electoral rolls and conduct of elections to Parliament, state legislatures, and Presidential/VP elections.

questions.question: The Finance Commission is constituted under:
questions.explanation: Article 280: Finance Commission is constituted by the President every 5 years to recommend distribution of tax revenues between Centre and States and grants-in-aid to states.

questions.question: The Union Public Service Commission (UPSC) is established by:
questions.explanation: Article 315 establishes the UPSC. It conducts examinations for appointments to the services of the Union. Its chairman is appointed by the President.

questions.question: The Comptroller and Auditor General of India (CAG) is established under:
questions.explanation: Article 148: CAG is a constitutional authority — controls the finances of India. CAG audits the accounts of Union and States. Reports are laid before Parliament/state legislatures.

questions.question: The National Commission for Scheduled Castes is a:
questions.explanation: Article 338 establishes the National Commission for Scheduled Castes as a constitutional body. It investigates matters relating to constitutional and statutory safeguards for SCs.

questions.question: The 7th Schedule divides subjects into:
questions.explanation: 7th Schedule: (1) Union List (100 subjects) — Parliament only, (2) State List (61 subjects) — State Legislature, (3) Concurrent List (52 subjects) — Both; Parliament prevails in case of conflict.

questions.question: Which subject is in the Concurrent List?
questions.explanation: Education (Entry 25 of Concurrent List) was moved from State List to Concurrent List by the 42nd Amendment (1976). Both Parliament and State Legislatures can legislate on it.

questions.question: Residuary powers (unlisted subjects) vest with:
questions.explanation: Article 248: Residuary powers (subjects not listed in any List) vest with Parliament. This is different from USA where residuary powers vest with states.

questions.question: The concept of 'Inter-State Council' is provided in:
questions.explanation: Article 263 provides for an Inter-State Council for coordinating relations between states and between states and the Centre. The Inter-State Council was set up in 1990.

questions.question: How many types of majority are needed for Constitutional Amendments?
questions.explanation: Constitutional amendments require: (1) Simple majority (for some provisions like Article 5), (2) Special majority: 2/3rd present and voting + majority of total membership (most amendments), (3) Special majority + ratification by half the states.

questions.question: The Basic Structure Doctrine was established in:
questions.explanation: Kesavananda Bharati v. State of Kerala (1973): 13-judge bench held that Parliament can amend any part of the Constitution EXCEPT its 'Basic Structure'. This doctrine is a cornerstone of Indian constitutional law.

questions.question: The 86th Constitutional Amendment (2002) relates to:
options: Right to Free and Compulsory Education
questions.explanation: 86th Amendment (2002): Added Article 21A (Free and Compulsory Education for 6-14 years as Fundamental Right), amended Article 45 DPSP, and added 11th Fundamental Duty.

questions.question: The 101st Constitutional Amendment (2016) introduced:
questions.explanation: 101st Constitutional Amendment (September 2016) introduced the Goods and Services Tax (GST) — a comprehensive indirect tax replacing multiple central and state taxes.

questions.question: The 97th Constitutional Amendment (2011) related to:
questions.explanation: 97th Constitutional Amendment (2011) gave constitutional status to cooperative societies — added Part IXB (Articles 243ZH to 243ZT) and made right to form cooperatives a fundamental right.

questions.question: Part II of the Constitution (Articles 5-11) deals with:
questions.explanation: Part II (Articles 5-11) deals with Citizenship — at the commencement of the Constitution. The details of citizenship are governed by the Citizenship Act 1955.

questions.question: India follows which type of citizenship?
options: Single citizenship (no dual citizenship for states)
questions.explanation: India provides for single citizenship — every citizen is a citizen of India only (unlike USA where citizens have both national and state citizenship). This promotes national unity.

questions.question: The Overseas Citizenship of India (OCI) scheme was introduced in:
questions.explanation: OCI scheme was introduced in 2005 through amendment to the Citizenship Act 1955. It allows PIOs (Persons of Indian Origin) to live and work in India indefinitely (not full citizenship).

questions.question: Minimum age to vote in India was reduced to 18 years by:
questions.explanation: 61st Constitutional Amendment (1988) reduced the voting age from 21 to 18 years. The first election where 18-year-olds could vote was in 1989.

questions.question: The Electronic Voting Machine (EVM) was first used in India in:
questions.explanation: EVMs were first used experimentally in Kerala state assembly elections in 1982 (70 constituencies). Nationwide EVM use in Lok Sabha began in 2004.

questions.question: Model Code of Conduct comes into force:
options: From the date of announcement of election schedule
questions.explanation: Model Code of Conduct (MCC) comes into operation from the date of announcement of election schedule (by Election Commission) and remains in force until the completion of the election process.

questions.question: The first Speaker of Lok Sabha was:
questions.explanation: Ganesh Vasudev Mavalankar (G.V. Mavalankar) was the first Speaker of Lok Sabha (1952-1956). He is called the 'Father of the Lok Sabha'.

questions.question: The maximum number of members in Rajya Sabha is:
questions.explanation: Rajya Sabha: maximum 250 members — 238 elected (representatives of states and UTs) + 12 nominated by President for expertise in literature, science, art, social service.

questions.question: A money bill is defined in:
questions.explanation: Article 110 defines a Money Bill — a bill that deals only with matters specified in Article 110(1): taxes, borrowing by government, appropriation from Consolidated Fund, etc.

questions.question: The Consolidated Fund of India is governed by:
questions.explanation: Article 266: Consolidated Fund of India — all revenues, loans raised, and repayments received by the Government of India go into this fund. No money can be withdrawn except through Parliamentary appropriation.

questions.question: Contingency Fund of India is established under:
questions.explanation: Article 267: Contingency Fund of India enables the President to make advances to meet unforeseen expenditure (limit ₹500 crore). Parliament approval is later sought to recoup the amount.

questions.question: Public Accounts Committee (PAC) examines:
options: Audit reports of CAG on expenditure by government
questions.explanation: PAC examines the CAG's audit reports to ensure public money was spent as Parliament intended. The chairman of PAC is traditionally from the Opposition party.

questions.question: The Estimates Committee reviews:
questions.explanation: Estimates Committee (30 members, from Lok Sabha only) examines the Estimates submitted to Parliament to suggest economies, improvements in organization, efficiency, and administrative reform.

questions.question: The SC/ST Prevention of Atrocities Act was enacted in:
questions.explanation: Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes (Prevention of Atrocities) Act 1989 provides stringent punishment for crimes against SCs and STs. It was amended in 2018 to overcome dilution by Supreme Court.

questions.question: Article 16(4) allows:
options: Reservation in government jobs for backward classes
questions.explanation: Article 16(4): Nothing in Article 16 shall prevent the State from making provision for reservation of appointments for any backward class of citizens not adequately represented in services.

questions.question: The Mandal Commission recommended OBC reservation of:
questions.explanation: Mandal Commission (1979-80) recommended 27% reservation for OBCs (Other Backward Classes) in central government jobs. Implemented in 1990 by VP Singh government — total reservation with SC/ST became 49.5%.

questions.question: The EWS (Economically Weaker Sections) 10% reservation was introduced by:
questions.explanation: 103rd Constitutional Amendment (January 2019) introduced 10% reservation for Economically Weaker Sections (EWS) among general category — through new Articles 15(6) and 16(6).

questions.question: Indra Sawhney v. Union of India (1992) established:
questions.explanation: Indra Sawhney case (Mandal Commission case, 1992): SC upheld OBC reservation but: (a) 50% ceiling on reservations, (b) Excluded creamy layer from OBC reservation, (c) No reservation in promotions.

questions.question: The National SC/ST Commission is chaired by:
options: A member from SC/ST community appointed by President
questions.explanation: National Commission for Scheduled Castes (Art. 338) and SC/ST has a Chairperson, Vice-Chairperson and other members appointed by the President.

questions.question: The Chairman of NITI Aayog is:
questions.explanation: The Prime Minister is the ex-officio Chairman of NITI Aayog. It also has a CEO (appointed by PM), Vice Chairman, and Governing Council (all CMs and LGs of states/UTs).

questions.question: GST Council is chaired by:
questions.explanation: GST Council (Article 279A, inserted by 101st Amendment 2016) is chaired by the Union Finance Minister. Members include Finance Ministers of all states.

questions.question: The Chief Election Commissioner of India can be removed by:
options: Same process as a Supreme Court judge
questions.explanation: The Chief Election Commissioner can be removed only through the same process as a Supreme Court judge — by an address of both Houses of Parliament passed by special majority (Article 324).

questions.question: Which State has the highest number of seats in Lok Sabha?
questions.explanation: Uttar Pradesh has the highest number of Lok Sabha seats — 80 seats out of 543. Maharashtra (48) is second, West Bengal (42) third, Bihar (40) fourth.

questions.question: 'Rajya Sabha' literally means:
questions.explanation: 'Rajya Sabha' means 'Council of States' in Hindi. 'Lok Sabha' means 'House of the People'. Rajya Sabha represents the states/UTs of India in Parliament.

questions.question: A Starred Question in Parliament is:
options: An oral answer question with supplementaries
questions.explanation: A Starred Question (marked with *) requires an oral answer in the House and allows supplementary questions by the member. About 20 starred questions are listed per sitting day.

questions.question: The National Commission for Women was established in:
questions.explanation: National Commission for Women was established by the National Commission for Women Act 1990, constituted in January 1992. It safeguards rights and interests of women.

questions.question: Which article of the Constitution talks about abolition of titles?
questions.explanation: Article 18 abolishes titles: No title shall be conferred by the State. Citizens cannot accept titles from foreign governments without President's consent. Exceptions: Military/academic distinctions.

questions.question: The Oaths of Members of Parliament are administered by:
options: President or person appointed by President
questions.explanation: Members of Parliament take oath/affirmation before the President (or a person appointed by the President) before taking their seat in Parliament.

questions.question: A 'Private Member's Bill' is a bill introduced by:
options: Any member who is not a minister
questions.explanation: A Private Member's Bill is introduced by any member of Parliament who is NOT a minister. It gets limited time (Friday afternoons). Very few private members' bills have been passed.

questions.question: The term 'Sunset Clause' in legislation means:
options: The law automatically expires after a fixed period
questions.explanation: Sunset Clause in a law means it will automatically expire (cease to be in force) after a specified date or event, unless renewed by Parliament.

questions.question: Parliamentary Sovereignty in India means:
options: Parliament has supreme power within Constitutional limits
questions.explanation: Unlike UK's absolute Parliamentary sovereignty, India has limited parliamentary sovereignty — Parliament is supreme within constitutional limits. Courts can strike down laws violating the Constitution.

questions.question: Financial Emergency (Art 360) has been invoked in India:
questions.explanation: Financial Emergency (Art 360) has never been proclaimed in India. It can be proclaimed when the financial stability or credit of India or any State is threatened.

questions.question: Residuary powers in India are vested with:
options: Supreme Court decides case by case
questions.explanation: Article 248 vests residuary powers (subjects not listed in any of the three Lists of Schedule 7) exclusively with Parliament. This is unlike USA where residuary powers belong to States.

questions.question: The 74th Constitutional Amendment relates to:
questions.explanation: The 74th Amendment (1992) constitutionalized Urban Local Bodies — Nagar Panchayats, Municipal Councils, Municipal Corporations. It added Part IX-A (Arts 243P-243ZG) and the 12th Schedule.

questions.question: Who is the custodian of the Constitution?
questions.explanation: The Supreme Court of India is the custodian and final interpreter of the Constitution. It has the power of judicial review to strike down any law or executive action that violates the Constitution.

questions.question: The Union Public Service Commission (UPSC) is a:
questions.explanation: UPSC is a Constitutional body established under Article 315. It recruits civil servants for All India Services and Central Services. Its Chairperson can be removed only by Presidential order.

questions.question: Which Amendment introduced 10% reservation for Economically Weaker Sections (EWS)?
questions.explanation: The 103rd Constitutional Amendment (2019) added Arts 15(6) and 16(6), providing 10% reservation in educational institutions and government jobs for Economically Weaker Sections (EWS) of general category.

questions.question: Which Article deals with the official language of the Union?
questions.explanation: Article 343 states Hindi in Devanagari script shall be the official language of the Union. English was also continued as an official language by the Official Languages Act, 1963.

questions.question: The 8th Schedule of the Constitution currently recognizes how many languages?
questions.explanation: The 8th Schedule currently recognizes 22 languages. Originally 14 languages at Constitution's commencement; Sindhi (21st Amendment, 1967), Konkani, Manipuri, Nepali (71st Amendment, 1992), Bodo, Dogri, Maithili, Santhali (92nd Amendment, 2003) were added.

questions.question: The Anti-Defection Law is contained in which Schedule?
questions.explanation: The Anti-Defection Law is in the 10th Schedule, added by the 52nd Constitutional Amendment (1985). It disqualifies members of Parliament and State Legislatures for defecting from their party.

questions.question: The Preamble of the Indian Constitution was amended to add the words 'Socialist' and 'Secular' by:
questions.explanation: The 42nd Constitutional Amendment (1976) — during Emergency — added the words 'Socialist', 'Secular', and 'Integrity' to the Preamble. The Preamble has been amended only once.

questions.question: The 'Basic Structure Doctrine' of the Indian Constitution was propounded in:
options: Kesavananda Bharati v. State of Kerala (1973)
Minerva Mills v. Union of India (1980)
Sankari Prasad v. Union of India (1951)
questions.explanation: The Basic Structure Doctrine was laid down by a 13-judge bench in Kesavananda Bharati v. State of Kerala (1973). It held Parliament cannot amend the 'basic structure' of the Constitution.

questions.question: The Goods and Services Tax (GST) was introduced by the:
questions.explanation: The 101st Constitutional Amendment (2016) introduced GST — inserting Art 246A (concurrent power to levy GST), Art 269A (IGST), Art 279A (GST Council), and amending Schedule 6/7.

questions.question: Which writ literally means 'by what authority' and is used to challenge an appointment to a public office?
questions.explanation: Quo Warranto (meaning 'by what authority') is issued to inquire into the legality of claim of a person to a public office.

questions.question: Which article of the Constitution protects persons from double jeopardy?
questions.explanation: Article 20(2) provides that no person shall be prosecuted and punished for the same offence more than once — protection against double jeopardy.

questions.question: What is a 'Writ' in legal terms?
options: A court order requiring someone to do or stop doing something
questions.explanation: A writ is a formal written order issued by a court directing someone to perform or refrain from performing a specific act.

questions.question: The three organs of the Indian Government are:
options: Lok Sabha, Rajya Sabha, Supreme Court
questions.explanation: The three organs of government are: Legislature (makes laws), Executive (implements laws), and Judiciary (interprets laws).

questions.question: Who was the Chairman of the Constituent Assembly that drafted the Indian Constitution?
questions.explanation: Dr. Rajendra Prasad was the President (Chairman) of the Constituent Assembly. Dr. B.R. Ambedkar was the Chairman of the Drafting Committee.

questions.question: What are the three types of Government in India?
questions.explanation: India has three tiers of government: Central Government, State Government, and Local Self-Government (Panchayats and Municipalities).

questions.question: In a democracy, the ultimate power rests with:
questions.explanation: In a democracy, ultimate sovereignty rests with the people, who exercise power through their elected representatives.

questions.question: Which article of the Constitution prohibits child labour (below 14 years) in factories and hazardous employment?
questions.explanation: Article 24 prohibits employment of children below 14 years in factories, mines, or other hazardous employment.

questions.question: What does 'Republic' mean in Indian context (as in 'Sovereign Democratic Republic')?
options: The head of state is elected, not hereditary
India has no head of state
India's PM is elected by people
questions.explanation: Republic means the head of state (President) is elected by the people's representatives, not hereditary like a monarch.

questions.question: The Parliament of India is located in:
questions.explanation: The Parliament House of India is located in New Delhi, the capital of India.

questions.question: The official language of the Union of India is:
questions.explanation: Article 343: Hindi in Devanagari script is the official language of the Union. English continues to be used for official purposes under Article 343(2). India has no 'national language'.

questions.question: The official language Hindi was to replace English by:
questions.explanation: Article 343(2) provided that English would continue for 15 years until 1965. But due to protests (especially in Tamil Nadu), the Official Languages Act 1963 made English continue indefinitely.

questions.question: Tamil is classified as a 'Classical Language' since:
questions.explanation: The Government of India recognized Tamil as the first Classical Language of India in 2004. Subsequently, Sanskrit, Telugu, Kannada, Malayalam, and Odia also received classical language status.

questions.question: Which states in India have a bicameral legislature (two houses)?
options: UP, Maharashtra, Bihar, Karnataka, Andhra Pradesh, Telangana, J&K
questions.explanation: States with Legislative Council (Vidhan Parishad): UP, Maharashtra, Karnataka, Andhra Pradesh, Telangana, Bihar. J&K had bicameral legislature before Art 370 abrogation.

questions.question: The maximum strength of a State Legislative Assembly is:
questions.explanation: Article 170: State Legislative Assembly (Vidhan Sabha) can have not more than 500 members and not less than 60 members (except Goa, Sikkim, Mizoram, Nagaland have less than 60).

questions.question: The National Human Rights Commission (NHRC) was established by:
questions.explanation: NHRC was established by the Protection of Human Rights Act 1993. The Chairman of NHRC is a retired Chief Justice of India. It investigates human rights violations.

questions.question: NOTA (None of the Above) option on EVMs was introduced in:
questions.explanation: NOTA was introduced following a Supreme Court order (PUCL case, 2013). Voters can press the NOTA button to reject all candidates while exercising their right to vote.

questions.question: The 'No Confidence Motion' must be passed by:
questions.explanation: A No Confidence Motion (Vote of No Confidence) against the government must be passed by a simple majority of Lok Sabha members present and voting. If passed, the PM must resign.

questions.question: Part IV-A of the Constitution deals with:
questions.explanation: Part IV-A (Article 51A) contains Fundamental Duties, added by the 42nd Constitutional Amendment (1976) on the recommendation of the Swaran Singh Committee.

questions.question: The Sarkaria Commission (1983) was set up to examine:
questions.explanation: Sarkaria Commission (1983-87) was set up to examine and review Centre-State relations. It submitted its report in 1988 with 247 recommendations to strengthen cooperative federalism.

questions.question: Union Territories are administered by:
questions.explanation: Union Territories are administered by the President of India (Article 239) through a Lieutenant Governor (LG) or Administrator appointed by the President.

questions.question: The Preamble calls India a 'Republic' which means:
options: Head of state is elected, not hereditary
questions.explanation: 'Republic' in the Preamble means the head of state (President) is elected for a fixed term, not hereditary. This distinguishes India from constitutional monarchies like UK.

questions.question: Article 368 deals with:
questions.explanation: Article 368 (Part XX) provides for the amendment of the Constitution. Parliament can amend any provision of the Constitution (subject to the Basic Structure doctrine established in Kesavananda Bharati case).

questions.question: India is a 'Sovereign' state which means:
options: India is free from external control and internal control is with the people
questions.explanation: Sovereign means India is an independent state — externally it is free from foreign domination, internally the people are the ultimate authority through their elected representatives.

questions.question: Who is considered the 'Father of the Indian Constitution'?
questions.explanation: Dr. B.R. Ambedkar played a pivotal role in drafting the Indian Constitution. He chaired the Drafting Committee.

questions.question: Which of the following amendments removed the Right to Property from the list of Fundamental Rights and made it a legal right?
questions.explanation: The 44th Amendment Act, 1978, repealed Article 19(1)(f) and Article 31, removing the Right to Property from Fundamental Rights and placing it in Article 300A as a legal right.

questions.question: The concept of 'Concurrent List' in the Indian Constitution is borrowed from the Constitution of which country?
questions.explanation: The Concurrent List, which includes subjects on which both the Union and State governments can legislate, is a feature borrowed from the Australian Constitution.

questions.question: According to the Indian Constitution, who has the power to declare a particular area as a 'Scheduled Area'?
options: The Governor of the concerned State
questions.explanation: The President of India is empowered to declare any area as a 'Scheduled Area' under the Fifth Schedule of the Constitution.

questions.question: Which of the following writs is issued by a high court or the Supreme Court to command a public authority to perform a public duty?
questions.explanation: Mandamus is a writ issued to compel a public official or body to perform a duty that they are legally required to do.

questions.question: The Preamble to the Indian Constitution was amended by which of the following Constitutional Amendment Acts?
questions.explanation: The Preamble was amended only once by the 42nd Constitutional Amendment Act, 1976, which added the words 'Socialist', 'Secular', and 'Integrity'.

questions.question:
Consider the following statements regarding the amendment procedure for the Indian Constitution:

1. A bill for the amendment of the Constitution can be introduced only in the Lok Sabha.
2. The President's prior recommendation is mandatory for introducing a Constitutional Amendment Bill involving changes to the federal structure.
3. Ratification by at least half of the state legislatures is required for amendments pertaining to the election of the President and the extent of the executive power of the Union.

Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanation: Statement 1 is incorrect as an amendment bill can be introduced in either House. Statement 2 is incorrect as no prior recommendation of the President is required.

questions.question:
Which of the following scenarios would necessitate a special majority of Parliament along with the ratification by half of the state legislatures for a constitutional amendment?

I. Alteration of the boundaries of states.
II. Changes to the Seventh Schedule (distribution of legislative powers).
III. Amendment to the procedure for the election of the President.
IV. Inclusion of a new language in the Eighth Schedule.

Select the correct option:
questions.explanation: Alteration of state boundaries (I) requires simple majority. Inclusion of a new language (IV) requires special majority only. The other two (II and III) require ratification by states.

questions.question:
A constitutional amendment that seeks to curtail the powers of the Supreme Court under Article 32 (Right to Constitutional Remedies) would require:

1. A special majority of Parliament (two-thirds of members present and voting, and more than half of the total membership).
2. Ratification by at least half of the state legislatures.
3. Assent of the President, which cannot be withheld or returned for reconsideration.

Which of the above conditions are necessary for such an amendment?
questions.explanation: Curtailing Article 32 affects the federal structure (judicial powers) and thus requires state ratification. The President's assent is mandatory, but he can return it for reconsideration.

questions.question: Which of the following schedules of the Indian Constitution deals with the allocation of seats in the Rajya Sabha to the states and union territories?
questions.explanation: The Fourth Schedule of the Indian Constitution details the allocation of seats for each state and union territory in the Rajya Sabha. The First Schedule deals with states and union territories, Second with emoluments, and Third with oaths.

questions.question: A Money Bill in the Indian Parliament can be introduced only in the Lok Sabha. Which article of the Constitution primarily deals with the definition of a Money Bill?
questions.explanation: Article 110 of the Indian Constitution defines what constitutes a Money Bill. While Article 109 deals with the procedure for Money Bills, Article 110 provides its definition.

questions.question: The power to amend the Constitution of India is vested in the Parliament. Which of the following constitutional amendments introduced the Ninth Schedule to bypass judicial review?
questions.explanation: The First Amendment Act of 1951 introduced the Ninth Schedule to protect certain laws from judicial review, primarily related to agrarian reforms. The 42nd Amendment is known as the 'Mini Constitution'.

questions.question: The President of India can issue ordinances when both Houses of Parliament are not in session. These ordinances have the same force and effect as an Act of Parliament. Under which article can the President exercise this power?
questions.explanation: Article 123 of the Indian Constitution grants the President the power to promulgate ordinances. Article 124 deals with the establishment of the Supreme Court.

questions.question: Which of the following DPSP (Directive Principles of State Policy) was added to the Constitution by the 42nd Amendment Act, 1976?
options: Equal justice and free legal aid
Uniform civil code for the citizens
Promotion of international peace and security
questions.explanation: Article 39A, concerning equal justice and free legal aid, was added by the 42nd Amendment Act, 1976. Organisation of village panchayats is Article 40, present from the beginning.

questions.question:
Consider the following statements regarding the amendment procedure under Article 368 of the Indian Constitution:
1. A bill for the amendment of the Constitution can be introduced in either House of Parliament.
2. The President's prior recommendation is mandatory for introducing a Constitutional Amendment Bill.
3. Ratification by at least half of the State Legislatures is required for amendments affecting the federal structure.
4. The President can withhold assent to a Constitutional Amendment Bill.

Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanation: Statement 2 is incorrect as no prior recommendation of the President is required. Statement 4 is incorrect as the President is bound to give assent to a Constitutional Amendment Bill.

questions.question:
Which of the following scenarios would necessitate a special majority of Parliament and ratification by at least half of the State Legislatures for a constitutional amendment?

I. Alteration of the boundaries of a state.
II. Amendment of the Seventh Schedule (distribution of legislative powers).
III. Procedure for election of the President.
IV. Fundamental Duties.

Select the correct option:
questions.explanation: Alteration of state boundaries (Article 3) requires a simple majority. Fundamental Duties can be amended by a special majority of Parliament only. The other two scenarios involve federal provisions requiring state ratification.

questions.question:
The concept of 'Basic Structure' of the Indian Constitution, as propounded by the Supreme Court, is primarily aimed at:

1. Preventing parliamentary overreach in amending fundamental rights.
2. Ensuring the supremacy of the judiciary over the legislature.
3. Preserving the core democratic and federal features of the Constitution.
4. Limiting the scope of judicial review of constitutional amendments.

Which of the above objectives are consistent with the 'Basic Structure' doctrine?
questions.explanation: The Basic Structure doctrine prevents Parliament from altering fundamental features of the Constitution. It does not aim to establish judicial supremacy or limit judicial review, but rather to define its boundaries in amendment cases.
